entities.description: Electronic encoding of Zupitza's printed pp. 6-25, beginning partway through printed p. 6 and ending before the notes section on p. 25. It preserves Fables I-IV and VII, German prefatory prose for Fable VII, notes, typography, printed pagination, and manuscript-folio milestones while omitting printed pp. 1-5, the opening prose on p. 6, Fables V-VI, and the remainder of pp. 25-28.
entities.label: University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English item CME00105

rights.basis: No item-level reuse statement was inspectable during the current Cloudflare challenge. Rights in the medieval text do not settle rights in modern encoding and markup.

bibliographic.citation: Julius Zupitza, 'Zu Lydgates Isopus,' Archiv für das Studium der neueren Sprachen und Litteraturen 85, XLIV. Jahrgang (Braunschweig: George Westermann, 1890), 1-28.
bibliographic.container_title: Archiv für das Studium der neueren Sprachen und Litteraturen
bibliographic.title: Zu Lydgates Isopus

entities.description: A twenty-eight-page article. Section I reports Zupitza's direct inspection of Harley 2251; section II prints the Trinity texts and collates Harley readings; section III prints Ashmole Fable VII from a transcription supplied by Napier and collates Harley; section IV supplies further London-only notes. Zupitza normalizes capitalization and some word division, marks expansions in italics, suppresses certain manuscript strokes, and presents substantive London variants in footnotes.
entities.label: Julius Zupitza, Zu Lydgates Isopus (1890)

rights.basis: The article was published in 1890; current U.S. duration guidance places such publication outside copyright.
rights.component: 1890 article text, edition, and transcription

entities.description: A paper anthology of English poems by John Lydgate and others, produced across the fifteenth and sixteenth centuries. Fables I-IV occupy ff. 12ra-16ra; the fourth stops after three stanzas and most of f. 16 is blank. Later John Stow leaves at ff. 235-237 contain two other fables but are not direct sources for CME00105.

holding.notes: Owned by John Stow and given by George Wilmer.

entities.label: Trinity College Cambridge MS R.3.19

notes: Fables I-IV are ff. 12ra-16ra.
The separately copied Stow fables at ff. 235-237 are outside CME00105's direct-source boundary.

entities.description: A composite paper codex of three separately produced units. Part I, ff. 1-133, is in John Shirley's hand and dated 1447-1456. Item 7 at ff. 24v-25r contains only the four stanzas of Fable VII, with a rubric naming John Lydgate and Oxford.

holding.notes: Ashmole bequest; transferred to the Bodleian in 1860.

entities.description: A London paper manuscript dated 1460-1483, copied by the Hammond Scribe, perhaps John Multon, apparently for John Vale. It is the only surviving medieval witness containing all seven Isopes Fabules, at ff. 257r-270v, though Wheatley notes a missing leaf corresponding to lines 267-322.

holding.notes: The Hammond Scribe and John Multon identifications and commission for John Vale remain qualified catalog attributions.

entities.label: British Library Harley MS 2251

entities.description: A historically attested but presently unlocated working transcription of MS. Ashmole 59, ff. 24v-25r. Zupitza states that he owed the transcription to his friend Napier. Same-volume and authority evidence strongly identifies him as Arthur Sampson Napier; the transcript's format, date, present location, and survival are unknown.
entities.label: Arthur Sampson Napier's working transcription of Ashmole 59 Fable VII

lineage.summary: CME00105 is a repository copy of the University of Michigan encoding of selected pages from Julius Zupitza's 1890 article 'Zu Lydgates Isopus.' The XML encodes Fables I-IV from Trinity College Cambridge MS R.3.19 and Fable VII from Bodleian MS Ashmole 59; the latter reached Zupitza through a working transcription supplied by Arthur Sampson Napier. Zupitza collated corresponding readings from British Library Harley MS 2251. The graph keeps the one seven-fable abstract work, five-fable selection, three witnesses, mediated transcript, edition, encoding, repository artifact, facsimiles, catalogs, and later edition distinct.

catalogSummary.author: John Lydgate
catalogSummary.date: Possibly before 1400; exact composition date and unity unresolved
catalogSummary.region: England; Oxford is securely associated only with Fable VII's manuscript rubric
catalogSummary.title: Isopes Fabules

catalogingSubject.label: Isopes Fabules (Fables I-IV and VII)
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The cataloged subject is one Middle English seven-fable work by John Lydgate, represented here by Zupitza's edited selection of Fables I-IV from Trinity College Cambridge MS R.3.19 and Fable VII from Bodleian Library MS Ashmole 59. Fable IV is a three-stanza witness fragment. Fables V and VI are absent from the XML. Zupitza's German preface and notes belong to the source representation, not to the medieval work's language, form, or extent; manuscripts, editions, encodings, facsimiles, and the mediated Ashmole transcript remain distinct lineage layers.

contentParts.label: Fable I: The Cock and the Precious Stone
contentParts.summary: A cock rejects a jewel he cannot use; the expanded moral values wisdom over material wealth and includes the collection prologue and a twenty-one-line envoy.

contentParts.label: Fable II: The Wolf and the Lamb
contentParts.summary: A wolf manufactures accusations against an innocent lamb and kills it, supporting a moral critique of tyranny and false pretexts.

contentParts.label: Fable III: The Frog and the Mouse
contentParts.summary: A deceitful frog tries to drown a mouse; a kite takes both, turning treachery against its perpetrator.

contentParts.label: Fable IV: The Hound and the Sheep (fragment)
notes: Trinity R.3.19 stops after three stanzas; the abstract fable is not thereby a twenty-one-line complete work.
contentParts.summary: The surviving three stanzas begin a false-witness dispute in which a hound makes a fraudulent claim against a sheep.

contentParts.label: Fable VII: The Hound and the Cheese
notes: Zupitza printed this text from a working transcription supplied by Napier, not from a claimed direct inspection of Ashmole 59.
contentParts.summary: A dog carrying stolen cheese sees its reflection, grasps for the apparent second piece, and loses what it had; the moral condemns covetousness and ill-gotten gain.

notes: Wheatley reports clues that may suggest a date before 1400 but rejects unsupported precision; manuscript dates are not composition dates.

dateStatements.display: 1460-1490 for the represented Trinity copy of Fables I-IV
1447-1456 for Ashmole 59 Part I; Wheatley dates the Fable VII copying to 1455/56

extent.description: The cataloged medieval selection has five fable sections, 82 LG elements, and 574 L elements; each verse group contains seven lines. Fables I-IV have 224, 133, 168, and 21 lines, and Fable VII has 28 independently numbered lines. The XML additionally has one German prose preface, 318 editorial notes, 223 manuscript-folio milestones, and 20 PB elements for printed pp. 6-25; those representation metrics are not added to the medieval-work extent.

formStatements.description: Rhyme royal stanzas, normally seven decasyllabic lines with ababbcc rhyme. The represented selection has 82 seven-line stanza groups and 574 lines.

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: The encoding also contains Zupitza's German editorial prose and notes, which are outside the abstract medieval work and therefore not cataloged as a work language.
DIMEV record 6701 is the decisive catalog boundary: one seven-fable abstract work represented by a five-fable edited selection, not five unrelated works.

DIMEV's Harley source note appears to describe only four stanzas of Fable VII even though the current BL catalog, the record's full locus and boundary lines, and Wheatley support a complete seven-fable Harley witness; the discrepancy is retained as a review caveat.
The German prose preface and 318 notes are source-representation features and are excluded from the medieval work's 574-line extent.

next_steps: Consult post-2013 scholarship and collate all three witnesses.
Keep the Oxford rubric scoped to Fable VII.
Distinguish DIMEV's useful one-work catalog boundary from proof of authorial assembly or order.
openQuestions.question: When and where were the seven fables composed, in what order, and how secure is their authorial unity?

next_steps: Compare each fable against the proposed French and Latin sources.
Do not collapse source-family proximity into one identified exemplar.
openQuestions.question: Which physical exemplars and textual routes from Marie de France, Gualterus Anglicus, and the Romulus tradition underlie each fable?

next_steps: Search Zupitza and Napier papers and correspondence.
Compare Zupitza's text with a new image-based transcription of Ashmole 59, ff. 24v-25r.
openQuestions.question: Does Arthur Sampson Napier's working transcription of Ashmole 59 survive, and can its exact mediation of Zupitza's Fable VII text be reconstructed?

next_steps: Ask Bodleian and the British Library first about existing complete surrogates, then request whole-object quotations if needed.
Recheck the BL legacy digitization and U-M item after service barriers clear.
Record image-level publication and reuse terms without transferring licenses between layers.
openQuestions.question: Can complete public or repository-supplied facsimiles of Ashmole 59 and Harley 2251 be obtained, and what exact reuse terms apply to them and to the U-M encoding?

notes: The Ashmole rubric says this fable was made at Oxford; it does not securely locate the entire collection.
The collection adapts Aesopic material; its precise source exemplars and routes remain unresolved.

scope.description: 1890 immediate source article and its selected fables
Selected text collated against corresponding Harley MS 2251 readings

notes: The surname is explicit; the full-name resolution is inferential.

scope.description: Working transcription of MS Ashmole 59, ff. 24v-25r supplied to Zupitza

summaries.value: John Lydgate's Isopes Fabules recasts Aesopic animal narratives in rhyme royal, expanding brief tales into moral arguments about wisdom, sufficiency, tyranny, deceit, and social justice. CME00105 preserves five selected textual units: the cock and precious stone, wolf and lamb, frog and mouse, a three-stanza fragment of the hound and sheep, and the hound with stolen cheese. The first four derive directly from Trinity R.3.19; the last reaches Zupitza through a transcription supplied by Arthur Sampson Napier from Ashmole 59. Harley 2251, the only medieval witness containing all seven fables, supplies collateral readings.

titles.supplied_by: Harley MS 2251 catalog and DIMEV normalized title

notes: Normalized discovery form; witness spellings vary.
titles.value: Wisdom is more of price than gold in coffers

notes: The Oxford claim is scoped to this rubric and must not be transferred automatically to the entire seven-fable collection.
titles.value: Here begynneþe a notable proverbe of ysopus Ethiopyene in balad by dann Johan Lidegate made in Oxenford

work.author: John Lydgate
work.dateDisplay: Possibly before 1400; exact composition date and unity unresolved

publication.embeddedTitle: The tale of the cok, that founde a precyous stone, groundyd by Isopus, the phylosopher of Rome, that yche man shuld take in gree suche, as god sent.

work.regionDisplay: England; Oxford is securely associated only with Fable VII's manuscript rubric
work.summary: John Lydgate's Isopes Fabules recasts Aesopic animal narratives in rhyme royal, expanding brief tales into moral arguments about wisdom, sufficiency, tyranny, deceit, and social justice. CME00105 preserves five selected textual units: the cock and precious stone, wolf and lamb, frog and mouse, a three-stanza fragment of the hound and sheep, and the hound with stolen cheese. The first four derive directly from Trinity R.3.19; the last reaches Zupitza through a transcription supplied by Arthur Sampson Napier from Ashmole 59. Harley 2251, the only medieval witness containing all seven fables, supplies collateral readings.
